revision.text:
[[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[co:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[co:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[sn:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11465812]] Wikidata name: Yamanoue Stele [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Yamanoue Stele |native_name =山ノ上碑 |alternate_name = |image =Yamanouehi.jpg |alt = |caption = Yamanoue stele | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Yamanoue Stele | map_size = 270px | relief = yes |coordinates = {{coord|36|16|38|N|139|01|40|E|display=inline}} | location = [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} | type = {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele}}, ''[[kofun]]'' | part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = 1.1 meters |builder = |material = {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} |built = 681AD |abandoned = |epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = | ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Stele'''|山ノ上碑|Yamanoue hi}} (also written 山上碑) is an {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele}} discovered in the Yamana neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The stele was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1921, and was raised in status to that of a Special National Historic Site in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/520 |title= 山上碑及び古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs (Japan) |language=ja |access-date= 11 August 2020}} It is associated with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Kofun'''|山ノ上古墳||}}, a ''[[kofun]]'' Kofun, which is also covered under the Special Historical Site designation. As one of the "{{ill|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|en|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|WD=Q42426141|lt=Three Stelae of Kōzuke}}", the Yamanoue Stele was submitted by Japan for inclusion into the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|Memory of the World Programme|en|Memory of the World Programme|WD=Q473858|lt=Memory of the World Programme}} in 2017 {{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/en/communication-and-information/memory-of-the-world/register/full-list-of-registered-heritage/registered-heritage-page-2/three-cherished-stelae-of-ancient-kozuke/ |title= Three Cherished Stelae of Ancient Kozuke|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The inscription on the Yamanoue Stele is the oldest example of writing in Chinese characters according to Japanese grammar. ==The Yamanoue Stele== The practice of erecting stelae was introduced to Japan from the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}} around the seventh century AD; however, the practice of erecting stelae never became as widespread as in China and Korea, partly due to the fact that in this period of Japanese history, writing was the preserve of the elite. There are only eighteen stelae known to exist from the seventh to eleventh centuries in Japan, of which three are located a small, three-kilometer, area of southwestern Gunma Prefecture. From the fifth century AD, this region welcomed immigrants from the mainland, who brought advanced technologies, such as raising and breeding horses, iron working, stoneware production, and weaving. It is likely that the local inhabitants regarded their region as sophisticated in comparison with surrounding provinces despite its distance from the capital at {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka}} or {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}.{{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/fileadmin/MULTIMEDIA/HQ/CI/CI/pdf/mow/nomination_forms/japan_stelae_eng.pdf|title= Nomination form International Memory of the World Registry|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The Yamanoue stele is a natural stone, only slightly worked, reminiscent of similar contemporary stone monuments which have been found in the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}. The presence of immigrants from Silla in this area and during this time is attested to in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' official chronicles. The monument is made of {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} with a height of 1.11 meters, width of 47 to 50 centimeters and a thickness of approximately. 50 centimeters. The inscription is engraved in {{ill|Clerical script|en|Clerical script|WD=Q1073960|lt=Clerical script}} and consists of 53 ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}'' arranged in four vertical rows. It is believed to be an epitaph for the neighboring ''kofun''. From the text it appears that this stele was commissioned by a Buddhist monk named Nagatoshi-no-hoshi from the temple of Hōkō-ji. This monk was a descendant of the local aristocrat who managed the Sano imperial estate, and may be related to the Miyake family mentioned in the nearby {{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}}. The monument was erected in honor of his late mother Kurome-tōji. The inscription gives the names of his ancestors on his mother's and father's side, and the date of 681 AD. While the inscription is written in Chinese characters (''kanji''), the text is according to {{ill|Japanese grammar|en|Japanese grammar|WD=Q1343874|lt=Japanese grammar}}, and is thus one of the earliest known examples of the adoption of ''kanji'' for use in written Japanese. {{Quotation|{{lang| ja |辛巳歳集月三日記
佐野三家定賜健守命孫黒賣刀自此
新川臣兒斯多々彌足尼孫大兒臣娶生兒
長利僧母爲記定文也　放光寺僧}}}} This stela is listed as being housed in a temple [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Kannon Bosatsu]] by in an inventory compiled by {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu}} in the 1790s. In 1884, the governor of Gunma Prefecture, Katori Motohiko, authorized the prefectural government to purchase a land where the stele is located, put the monument on a new base stone, and had a small structure built to protect it. The current structure dates from 1992, and the stele can be viewed by the public through a window. It is a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Nishi-Yamana Station|en|Nishi-Yamana Station|WD=Q11627819|lt=Nishi-Yamana Station}} or {{ill|Yamana Station|en|Yamana Station|WD=Q11467648|lt=Yamana Station}} on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu|Jōshin Dentetsu]] railway. == Family tree == {{familytree/start|style=float:left; font-size:80%; margin:1em;}} {{familytree| 01 | | 02 |01=Tatami-no-sukune
{{lang| ja |多々彌足尼}}|02=Takemori-no-mikoto
{{lang| ja |健守命}}}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 03 | | 04 |03=Niikawa-no-omi
{{lang| ja |新川臣}}|04=unknown}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 05 |y| 06 |05=Ōgo-no-omi
{{lang| ja |大兒臣}} |06=Kurome-no-toji
{{lang| ja |黒賣刀自}}}} {{familytree| | | |!| | | }} {{familytree| | | 07 | | |07=Chōri-no-hōshi
{{lang| ja |長利僧}}}} {{familytree/end}}
==The Yamanoue Kofun== The Yamanoue Kofun is a typical late [[Kofun period]] domed tumulus, located about 15 meters on the east side of the Yamanoue Stele. It has a diameter of 15 meters. Neither ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' nor ''[[fukiishi]]'' have been found at the site. The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

revision.text:
[[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[co:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[co:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[sn:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11465812]] Wikidata name: Yamanoue Stele [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Yamanoue Stele |native_name =山ノ上碑 |alternate_name = |image =Yamanouehi.jpg |alt = |caption = Yamanoue stele | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Yamanoue Stele | map_size = 270px | relief = yes |coordinates = {{coord|36|16|38|N|139|01|40|E|display=inline}} | location = [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} | type = {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele}}, ''[[kofun]]'' | part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = 1.1 meters |builder = |material = {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} |built = 681AD |abandoned = |epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = | ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Stele'''|山ノ上碑|Yamanoue hi}} (also written 山上碑) is an {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele}} discovered in the Yamana neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The stele was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1921, and was raised in status to that of a Special National Historic Site in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/520 |title= 山上碑及び古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs (Japan) |language=ja |access-date= 11 August 2020}} It is associated with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Kofun'''|山ノ上古墳||}}, a ''[[kofun]]'' Kofun, which is also covered under the Special Historical Site designation. As one of the "{{ill|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|en|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|WD=Q42426141|lt=Three Stelae of Kōzuke}}", the Yamanoue Stele was submitted by Japan for inclusion into the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|Memory of the World Programme|en|Memory of the World Programme|WD=Q473858|lt=Memory of the World Programme}} in 2017 {{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/en/communication-and-information/memory-of-the-world/register/full-list-of-registered-heritage/registered-heritage-page-2/three-cherished-stelae-of-ancient-kozuke/ |title= Three Cherished Stelae of Ancient Kozuke|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The inscription on the Yamanoue Stele is the oldest example of writing in Chinese characters according to Japanese grammar. ==The Yamanoue Stele== The practice of erecting stelae was introduced to Japan from the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}} around the seventh century AD; however, the practice of erecting stelae never became as widespread as in China and Korea, partly due to the fact that in this period of Japanese history, writing was the preserve of the elite. There are only eighteen stelae known to exist from the seventh to eleventh centuries in Japan, of which three are located a small, three-kilometer, area of southwestern Gunma Prefecture. From the fifth century AD, this region welcomed immigrants from the mainland, who brought advanced technologies, such as raising and breeding horses, iron working, stoneware production, and weaving. It is likely that the local inhabitants regarded their region as sophisticated in comparison with surrounding provinces despite its distance from the capital at {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka}} or {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}.{{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/fileadmin/MULTIMEDIA/HQ/CI/CI/pdf/mow/nomination_forms/japan_stelae_eng.pdf|title= Nomination form International Memory of the World Registry|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The Yamanoue stele is a natural stone, only slightly worked, reminiscent of similar contemporary stone monuments which have been found in the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}. The presence of immigrants from Silla in this area and during this time is attested to in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' official chronicles. The monument is made of {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} with a height of 1.11 meters, width of 47 to 50 centimeters and a thickness of approximately. 50 centimeters. The inscription is engraved in {{ill|Clerical script|en|Clerical script|WD=Q1073960|lt=Clerical script}} and consists of 53 ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}'' arranged in four vertical rows. It is believed to be an epitaph for the neighboring ''kofun''. From the text it appears that this stele was commissioned by a Buddhist monk named Nagatoshi-no-hoshi from the temple of Hōkō-ji. This monk was a descendant of the local aristocrat who managed the Sano imperial estate, and may be related to the Miyake family mentioned in the nearby {{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}}. The monument was erected in honor of his late mother Kurome-tōji. The inscription gives the names of his ancestors on his mother's and father's side, and the date of 681 AD. While the inscription is written in Chinese characters (''kanji''), the text is according to {{ill|Japanese grammar|en|Japanese grammar|WD=Q1343874|lt=Japanese grammar}}, and is thus one of the earliest known examples of the adoption of ''kanji'' for use in written Japanese. {{Quotation|{{lang| ja |辛巳歳集月三日記
佐野三家定賜健守命孫黒賣刀自此
新川臣兒斯多々彌足尼孫大兒臣娶生兒
長利僧母爲記定文也　放光寺僧}}}} This stela is listed as being housed in a temple [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Kannon Bosatsu]] by in an inventory compiled by {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu}} in the 1790s. In 1884, the governor of Gunma Prefecture, Katori Motohiko, authorized the prefectural government to purchase a land where the stele is located, put the monument on a new base stone, and had a small structure built to protect it. The current structure dates from 1992, and the stele can be viewed by the public through a window. It is a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Nishi-Yamana Station|en|Nishi-Yamana Station|WD=Q11627819|lt=Nishi-Yamana Station}} or {{ill|Yamana Station|en|Yamana Station|WD=Q11467648|lt=Yamana Station}} on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu|Jōshin Dentetsu]] railway. == Family tree == {{familytree/start|style=float:left; font-size:80%; margin:1em;}} {{familytree| 01 | | 02 |01=Tatami-no-sukune
{{lang| ja |多々彌足尼}}|02=Takemori-no-mikoto
{{lang| ja |健守命}}}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 03 | | 04 |03=Niikawa-no-omi
{{lang| ja |新川臣}}|04=unknown}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 05 |y| 06 |05=Ōgo-no-omi
{{lang| ja |大兒臣}} |06=Kurome-no-toji
{{lang| ja |黒賣刀自}}}} {{familytree| | | |!| | | }} {{familytree| | | 07 | | |07=Chōri-no-hōshi
{{lang| ja |長利僧}}}} {{familytree/end}}
==The Yamanoue Kofun== The Yamanoue Kofun is a typical late [[Kofun period]] domed tumulus, located about 15 meters on the east side of the Yamanoue Stele. It has a diameter of 15 meters. Neither ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' nor ''[[fukiishi]]'' have been found at the site. The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

==The Yamanoue Kofun== The Yamanoue Kofun is a typical late [[Kofun period]] domed tumulus, located about 15 meters on the east side of the Yamanoue Stele. It has a diameter of 15 meters. Neither ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' nor ''[[fukiishi]]'' have been found at the site. The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site |name = Yamanoue Stele |native_name =山ノ上碑 |alternate_name = |image =Yamanouehi.jpg |alt = |caption = Yamanoue stele | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Yamanoue Stele | map_size = 270px | relief = yes |coordinates = {{coord|36|16|38|N|139|01|40|E|display=inline}} | location = [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} | type = {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele}}, ''[[kofun]]'' | part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = 1.1 meters |builder = |material = {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} |built = 681AD |abandoned = |epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = | ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Stele'''|山ノ上碑|Yamanoue hi}} (also written 山上碑) is an {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}} {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele}} discovered in the Yamana neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The stele was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1921, and was raised in status to that of a Special National Historic Site in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/520 |title= 山上碑及び古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs (Japan) |language=ja |access-date= 11 August 2020}} It is associated with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Kofun'''|山ノ上古墳||}}, a ''[[kofun]]'' Kofun, which is also covered under the Special Historical Site designation. As one of the "{{ill|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|en|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|WD=Q42426141|lt=Three Stelae of Kōzuke}}", the Yamanoue Stele was submitted by Japan for inclusion into the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|Memory of the World Programme|en|Memory of the World Programme|WD=Q473858|lt=Memory of the World Programme}} in 2017 {{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/en/communication-and-information/memory-of-the-world/register/full-list-of-registered-heritage/registered-heritage-page-2/three-cherished-stelae-of-ancient-kozuke/ |title= Three Cherished Stelae of Ancient Kozuke|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The inscription on the Yamanoue Stele is the oldest example of writing in Chinese characters according to Japanese grammar. ==The Yamanoue Stele== The practice of erecting stelae was introduced to Japan from the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}} around the seventh century AD; however, the practice of erecting stelae never became as widespread as in China and Korea, partly due to the fact that in this period of Japanese history, writing was the preserve of the elite. There are only eighteen stelae known to exist from the seventh to eleventh centuries in Japan, of which three are located a small, three-kilometer, area of southwestern Gunma Prefecture. From the fifth century AD, this region welcomed immigrants from the mainland, who brought advanced technologies, such as raising and breeding horses, iron working, stoneware production, and weaving. It is likely that the local inhabitants regarded their region as sophisticated in comparison with surrounding provinces despite its distance from the capital at {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka}} or {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}.{{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/fileadmin/MULTIMEDIA/HQ/CI/CI/pdf/mow/nomination_forms/japan_stelae_eng.pdf|title= Nomination form International Memory of the World Registry|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The Yamanoue stele is a natural stone, only slightly worked, reminiscent of similar contemporary stone monuments which have been found in the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}. The presence of immigrants from Silla in this area and during this time is attested to in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' official chronicles. The monument is made of {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} with a height of 1.11 meters, width of 47 to 50 centimeters and a thickness of approximately. 50 centimeters. The inscription is engraved in {{ill|Clerical script|en|Clerical script|WD=Q1073960|lt=Clerical script}} and consists of 53 ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}'' arranged in four vertical rows. It is believed to be an epitaph for the neighboring ''kofun''. From the text it appears that this stele was commissioned by a Buddhist monk named Nagatoshi-no-hoshi from the temple of Hōkō-ji. This monk was a descendant of the local aristocrat who managed the Sano imperial estate, and may be related to the Miyake family mentioned in the nearby {{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}}. The monument was erected in honor of his late mother Kurome-tōji. The inscription gives the names of his ancestors on his mother's and father's side, and the date of 681 AD. While the inscription is written in Chinese characters (''kanji''), the text is according to {{ill|Japanese grammar|en|Japanese grammar|WD=Q1343874|lt=Japanese grammar}}, and is thus one of the earliest known examples of the adoption of ''kanji'' for use in written Japanese. {{Quotation|{{lang| ja |辛巳歳集月三日記
佐野三家定賜健守命孫黒賣刀自此
新川臣兒斯多々彌足尼孫大兒臣娶生兒
長利僧母爲記定文也　放光寺僧}}}} This stela is listed as being housed in a temple [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Kannon Bosatsu]] by in an inventory compiled by {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu}} in the 1790s. In 1884, the governor of Gunma Prefecture, Katori Motohiko, authorized the prefectural government to purchase a land where the stele is located, put the monument on a new base stone, and had a small structure built to protect it. The current structure dates from 1992, and the stele can be viewed by the public through a window. It is a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Nishi-Yamana Station|en|Nishi-Yamana Station|WD=Q11627819|lt=Nishi-Yamana Station}} or {{ill|Yamana Station|en|Yamana Station|WD=Q11467648|lt=Yamana Station}} on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu|Jōshin Dentetsu]] railway. == Family tree == {{familytree/start|style=float:left; font-size:80%; margin:1em;}} {{familytree| 01 | | 02 |01=Tatami-no-sukune
{{lang| ja |多々彌足尼}}|02=Takemori-no-mikoto
{{lang| ja |健守命}}}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 03 | | 04 |03=Niikawa-no-omi
{{lang| ja |新川臣}}|04=unknown}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 05 |y| 06 |05=Ōgo-no-omi
{{lang| ja |大兒臣}} |06=Kurome-no-toji
{{lang| ja |黒賣刀自}}}} {{familytree| | | |!| | | }} {{familytree| | | 07 | | |07=Chōri-no-hōshi
{{lang| ja |長利僧}}}} {{familytree/end}}
==The Yamanoue Kofun== The Yamanoue Kofun is a typical late [[Kofun period]] domed tumulus, located about 15 meters on the east side of the Yamanoue Stele. It has a diameter of 15 meters. Neither ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' nor ''[[fukiishi]]'' have been found at the site. The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture}}, in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The stele was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1921, and was raised in status to that of a Special National Historic Site in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/520 |title= 山上碑及び古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs (Japan) |language=ja |access-date= 11 August 2020}} It is associated with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Kofun'''|山ノ上古墳||}}, a ''[[kofun]]'' Kofun, which is also covered under the Special Historical Site designation. As one of the "{{ill|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|en|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|WD=Q42426141|lt=Three Stelae of Kōzuke}}", the Yamanoue Stele was submitted by Japan for inclusion into the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO}} {{ill|Memory of the World Programme|en|Memory of the World Programme|WD=Q473858|lt=Memory of the World Programme}} in 2017 {{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/en/communication-and-information/memory-of-the-world/register/full-list-of-registered-heritage/registered-heritage-page-2/three-cherished-stelae-of-ancient-kozuke/ |title= Three Cherished Stelae of Ancient Kozuke|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The inscription on the Yamanoue Stele is the oldest example of writing in Chinese characters according to Japanese grammar. ==The Yamanoue Stele== The practice of erecting stelae was introduced to Japan from the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China}} around the seventh century AD; however, the practice of erecting stelae never became as widespread as in China and Korea, partly due to the fact that in this period of Japanese history, writing was the preserve of the elite. There are only eighteen stelae known to exist from the seventh to eleventh centuries in Japan, of which three are located a small, three-kilometer, area of southwestern Gunma Prefecture. From the fifth century AD, this region welcomed immigrants from the mainland, who brought advanced technologies, such as raising and breeding horses, iron working, stoneware production, and weaving. It is likely that the local inhabitants regarded their region as sophisticated in comparison with surrounding provinces despite its distance from the capital at {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka}} or {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}.{{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/fileadmin/MULTIMEDIA/HQ/CI/CI/pdf/mow/nomination_forms/japan_stelae_eng.pdf|title= Nomination form International Memory of the World Registry|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The Yamanoue stele is a natural stone, only slightly worked, reminiscent of similar contemporary stone monuments which have been found in the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}. The presence of immigrants from Silla in this area and during this time is attested to in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' official chronicles. The monument is made of {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite}} with a height of 1.11 meters, width of 47 to 50 centimeters and a thickness of approximately. 50 centimeters. The inscription is engraved in {{ill|Clerical script|en|Clerical script|WD=Q1073960|lt=Clerical script}} and consists of 53 ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}'' arranged in four vertical rows. It is believed to be an epitaph for the neighboring ''kofun''. From the text it appears that this stele was commissioned by a Buddhist monk named Nagatoshi-no-hoshi from the temple of Hōkō-ji. This monk was a descendant of the local aristocrat who managed the Sano imperial estate, and may be related to the Miyake family mentioned in the nearby {{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}}. The monument was erected in honor of his late mother Kurome-tōji. The inscription gives the names of his ancestors on his mother's and father's side, and the date of 681 AD. While the inscription is written in Chinese characters (''kanji''), the text is according to {{ill|Japanese grammar|en|Japanese grammar|WD=Q1343874|lt=Japanese grammar}}, and is thus one of the earliest known examples of the adoption of ''kanji'' for use in written Japanese. {{Quotation|{{lang| ja |辛巳歳集月三日記
佐野三家定賜健守命孫黒賣刀自此
新川臣兒斯多々彌足尼孫大兒臣娶生兒
長利僧母爲記定文也　放光寺僧}}}} This stela is listed as being housed in a temple [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Kannon Bosatsu]] by in an inventory compiled by {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu}} in the 1790s. In 1884, the governor of Gunma Prefecture, Katori Motohiko, authorized the prefectural government to purchase a land where the stele is located, put the monument on a new base stone, and had a small structure built to protect it. The current structure dates from 1992, and the stele can be viewed by the public through a window. It is a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Nishi-Yamana Station|en|Nishi-Yamana Station|WD=Q11627819|lt=Nishi-Yamana Station}} or {{ill|Yamana Station|en|Yamana Station|WD=Q11467648|lt=Yamana Station}} on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu|Jōshin Dentetsu]] railway. == Family tree == {{familytree/start|style=float:left; font-size:80%; margin:1em;}} {{familytree| 01 | | 02 |01=Tatami-no-sukune
{{lang| ja |多々彌足尼}}|02=Takemori-no-mikoto
{{lang| ja |健守命}}}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 03 | | 04 |03=Niikawa-no-omi
{{lang| ja |新川臣}}|04=unknown}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 05 |y| 06 |05=Ōgo-no-omi
{{lang| ja |大兒臣}} |06=Kurome-no-toji
{{lang| ja |黒賣刀自}}}} {{familytree| | | |!| | | }} {{familytree| | | 07 | | |07=Chōri-no-hōshi
{{lang| ja |長利僧}}}} {{familytree/end}}
==The Yamanoue Kofun== The Yamanoue Kofun is a typical late [[Kofun period]] domed tumulus, located about 15 meters on the east side of the Yamanoue Stele. It has a diameter of 15 meters. Neither ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa}}'' nor ''[[fukiishi]]'' have been found at the site. The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

The presence of immigrants from Silla in this area and during this time is attested to in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' official chronicles. The monument is made of {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} with a height of 1.11 meters, width of 47 to 50 centimeters and a thickness of approximately. 50 centimeters. The inscription is engraved in {{ill|Clerical script|en|Clerical script|WD=Q1073960|lt=Clerical script|1=Clerical script}} and consists of 53 ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}'' arranged in four vertical rows. It is believed to be an epitaph for the neighboring ''kofun''. From the text it appears that this stele was commissioned by a Buddhist monk named Nagatoshi-no-hoshi from the temple of Hōkō-ji. This monk was a descendant of the local aristocrat who managed the Sano imperial estate, and may be related to the Miyake family mentioned in the nearby {{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele|1=Kanaizawa Stele}}. The monument was erected in honor of his late mother Kurome-tōji. The inscription gives the names of his ancestors on his mother's and father's side, and the date of 681 AD. While the inscription is written in Chinese characters (''kanji''), the text is according to {{ill|Japanese grammar|en|Japanese grammar|WD=Q1343874|lt=Japanese grammar|1=Japanese grammar}}, and is thus one of the earliest known examples of the adoption of ''kanji'' for use in written Japanese. {{Quotation|{{lang| ja |辛巳歳集月三日記
佐野三家定賜健守命孫黒賣刀自此
新川臣兒斯多々彌足尼孫大兒臣娶生兒
長利僧母爲記定文也　放光寺僧}}}} This stela is listed as being housed in a temple [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Kannon Bosatsu]] by in an inventory compiled by {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu|1=Matsudaira Sadanobu}} in the 1790s. In 1884, the governor of Gunma Prefecture, Katori Motohiko, authorized the prefectural government to purchase a land where the stele is located, put the monument on a new base stone, and had a small structure built to protect it. The current structure dates from 1992, and the stele can be viewed by the public through a window. It is a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Nishi-Yamana Station|en|Nishi-Yamana Station|WD=Q11627819|lt=Nishi-Yamana Station|1=Nishi-Yamana Station}} or {{ill|Yamana Station|en|Yamana Station|WD=Q11467648|lt=Yamana Station|1=Yamana Station}} on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu|Jōshin Dentetsu]] railway. == Family tree == {{familytree/start|style=float:left; font-size:80%; margin:1em;}} {{familytree| 01 | | 02 |01=Tatami-no-sukune

==The Yamanoue Kofun== The Yamanoue Kofun is a typical late [[Kofun period]] domed tumulus, located about 15 meters on the east side of the Yamanoue Stele. It has a diameter of 15 meters. Neither ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' nor ''[[fukiishi]]'' have been found at the site. The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele|1=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele|1=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

270px | relief = yes |coordinates = {{coord|36|16|38|N|139|01|40|E|display=inline}} | location = [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele|1=Stele}}, ''[[kofun]]'' | part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Stele'''|山ノ上碑|Yamanoue hi}} (also written 山上碑) is an {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele|1=Stele}} discovered in the Yamana neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The stele was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1921, and was raised in status to that of a Special National Historic Site in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/520 |title= 山上碑及び古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs (Japan) |language=ja |access-date= 11 August 2020}} It is associated with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Kofun'''|山ノ上古墳||}}, a ''[[kofun]]'' Kofun, which is also covered under the Special Historical Site designation. As one of the "{{ill|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|en|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|WD=Q42426141|lt=Three Stelae of Kōzuke|1=Three Stelae of Kōzuke}}", the Yamanoue Stele was submitted by Japan for inclusion into the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|Memory of the World Programme|en|Memory of the World Programme|WD=Q473858|lt=Memory of the World Programme|1=Memory of the World Programme}} in 2017 {{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/en/communication-and-information/memory-of-the-world/register/full-list-of-registered-heritage/registered-heritage-page-2/three-cherished-stelae-of-ancient-kozuke/ |title= Three Cherished Stelae of Ancient Kozuke|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The inscription on the Yamanoue Stele is the oldest example of writing in Chinese characters according to Japanese grammar. ==The Yamanoue Stele== The practice of erecting stelae was introduced to Japan from the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}} around the seventh century AD; however, the practice of erecting stelae never became as widespread as in China and Korea, partly due to the fact that in this period of Japanese history, writing was the preserve of the elite. There are only eighteen stelae known to exist from the seventh to eleventh centuries in Japan, of which three are located a small, three-kilometer, area of southwestern Gunma Prefecture. From the fifth century AD, this region welcomed immigrants from the mainland, who brought advanced technologies, such as raising and breeding horses, iron working, stoneware production, and weaving. It is likely that the local inhabitants regarded their region as sophisticated in comparison with surrounding provinces despite its distance from the capital at {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}} or {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}.{{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/fileadmin/MULTIMEDIA/HQ/CI/CI/pdf/mow/nomination_forms/japan_stelae_eng.pdf|title= Nomination form International Memory of the World Registry|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The Yamanoue stele is a natural stone, only slightly worked, reminiscent of similar contemporary stone monuments which have been found in the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. The presence of immigrants from Silla in this area and during this time is attested to in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' official chronicles. The monument is made of {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} with a height of 1.11 meters, width of 47 to 50 centimeters and a thickness of approximately. 50 centimeters. The inscription is engraved in {{ill|Clerical script|en|Clerical script|WD=Q1073960|lt=Clerical script|1=Clerical script}} and consists of 53 ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}'' arranged in four vertical rows. It is believed to be an epitaph for the neighboring ''kofun''. From the text it appears that this stele was commissioned by a Buddhist monk named Nagatoshi-no-hoshi from the temple of Hōkō-ji. This monk was a descendant of the local aristocrat who managed the Sano imperial estate, and may be related to the Miyake family mentioned in the nearby {{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele|1=Kanaizawa Stele}}. The monument was erected in honor of his late mother Kurome-tōji. The inscription gives the names of his ancestors on his mother's and father's side, and the date of 681 AD. While the inscription is written in Chinese characters (''kanji''), the text is according to {{ill|Japanese grammar|en|Japanese grammar|WD=Q1343874|lt=Japanese grammar|1=Japanese grammar}}, and is thus one of the earliest known examples of the adoption of ''kanji'' for use in written Japanese. {{Quotation|{{lang| ja |辛巳歳集月三日記
佐野三家定賜健守命孫黒賣刀自此
新川臣兒斯多々彌足尼孫大兒臣娶生兒
長利僧母爲記定文也　放光寺僧}}}} This stela is listed as being housed in a temple [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Kannon Bosatsu]] by in an inventory compiled by {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu|1=Matsudaira Sadanobu}} in the 1790s. In 1884, the governor of Gunma Prefecture, Katori Motohiko, authorized the prefectural government to purchase a land where the stele is located, put the monument on a new base stone, and had a small structure built to protect it. The current structure dates from 1992, and the stele can be viewed by the public through a window. It is a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Nishi-Yamana Station|en|Nishi-Yamana Station|WD=Q11627819|lt=Nishi-Yamana Station|1=Nishi-Yamana Station}} or {{ill|Yamana Station|en|Yamana Station|WD=Q11467648|lt=Yamana Station|1=Yamana Station}} on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu|Jōshin Dentetsu]] railway. == Family tree == {{familytree/start|style=float:left; font-size:80%; margin:1em;}} {{familytree| 01 | | 02 |01=Tatami-no-sukune

It is a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Nishi-Yamana Station|en|Nishi-Yamana Station|WD=Q11627819|lt=Nishi-Yamana Station|1=Nishi-Yamana Station}} or {{ill|Yamana Station|en|Yamana Station|WD=Q11467648|lt=Yamana Station|1=Yamana Station}} on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu|Jōshin Dentetsu]] railway. == Family tree == {{familytree/start|style=float:left; font-size:80%; margin:1em;}} {{familytree| 01 | | 02 |01=Tatami-no-sukune
{{lang| ja |多々彌足尼}}|02=Takemori-no-mikoto
{{lang| ja |健守命}}}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 03 | | 04 |03=Niikawa-no-omi
{{lang| ja |新川臣}}|04=unknown}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 05 |y| 06 |05=Ōgo-no-omi
{{lang| ja |大兒臣}} |06=Kurome-no-toji
{{lang| ja |黒賣刀自}}}} {{familytree| | | |!| | | }} {{familytree| | | 07 | | |07=Chōri-no-hōshi
{{lang| ja |長利僧}}}} {{familytree/end}}
==The Yamanoue Kofun== The Yamanoue Kofun is a typical late [[Kofun period]] domed tumulus, located about 15 meters on the east side of the Yamanoue Stele. It has a diameter of 15 meters. Neither ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' nor ''[[fukiishi]]'' have been found at the site. The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele|1=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele|1=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
[[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[co:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[co:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{Infobox ancient site |name = Yamanoue Stele |native_name =山ノ上碑 |alternate_name = |image =Yamanouehi.jpg |alt = |caption = Yamanoue stele | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Yamanoue Stele | map_size = 270px | relief = yes |coordinates = {{coord|36|16|38|N|139|01|40|E|display=inline}} | location = [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele|1=Stele}}, ''[[kofun]]'' | part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = 1.1 meters |builder = |material = {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} |built = 681AD |abandoned = |epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = | ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Stele'''|山ノ上碑|Yamanoue hi}} (also written 山上碑) is an {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele|1=Stele}} discovered in the Yamana neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The stele was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1921, and was raised in status to that of a Special National Historic Site in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/520 |title= 山上碑及び古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs (Japan) |language=ja |access-date= 11 August 2020}} It is associated with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Kofun'''|山ノ上古墳||}}, a ''[[kofun]]'' Kofun, which is also covered under the Special Historical Site designation. As one of the "{{ill|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|en|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|WD=Q42426141|lt=Three Stelae of Kōzuke|1=Three Stelae of Kōzuke}}", the Yamanoue Stele was submitted by Japan for inclusion into the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|Memory of the World Programme|en|Memory of the World Programme|WD=Q473858|lt=Memory of the World Programme|1=Memory of the World Programme}} in 2017 {{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/en/communication-and-information/memory-of-the-world/register/full-list-of-registered-heritage/registered-heritage-page-2/three-cherished-stelae-of-ancient-kozuke/ |title= Three Cherished Stelae of Ancient Kozuke|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The inscription on the Yamanoue Stele is the oldest example of writing in Chinese characters according to Japanese grammar. ==The Yamanoue Stele== The practice of erecting stelae was introduced to Japan from the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}} around the seventh century AD; however, the practice of erecting stelae never became as widespread as in China and Korea, partly due to the fact that in this period of Japanese history, writing was the preserve of the elite. There are only eighteen stelae known to exist from the seventh to eleventh centuries in Japan, of which three are located a small, three-kilometer, area of southwestern Gunma Prefecture. From the fifth century AD, this region welcomed immigrants from the mainland, who brought advanced technologies, such as raising and breeding horses, iron working, stoneware production, and weaving. It is likely that the local inhabitants regarded their region as sophisticated in comparison with surrounding provinces despite its distance from the capital at {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}} or {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}.{{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/fileadmin/MULTIMEDIA/HQ/CI/CI/pdf/mow/nomination_forms/japan_stelae_eng.pdf|title= Nomination form International Memory of the World Registry|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The Yamanoue stele is a natural stone, only slightly worked, reminiscent of similar contemporary stone monuments which have been found in the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. The presence of immigrants from Silla in this area and during this time is attested to in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' official chronicles. The monument is made of {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} with a height of 1.11 meters, width of 47 to 50 centimeters and a thickness of approximately. 50 centimeters. The inscription is engraved in {{ill|Clerical script|en|Clerical script|WD=Q1073960|lt=Clerical script|1=Clerical script}} and consists of 53 ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}'' arranged in four vertical rows. It is believed to be an epitaph for the neighboring ''kofun''. From the text it appears that this stele was commissioned by a Buddhist monk named Nagatoshi-no-hoshi from the temple of Hōkō-ji. This monk was a descendant of the local aristocrat who managed the Sano imperial estate, and may be related to the Miyake family mentioned in the nearby {{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele|1=Kanaizawa Stele}}. The monument was erected in honor of his late mother Kurome-tōji. The inscription gives the names of his ancestors on his mother's and father's side, and the date of 681 AD. While the inscription is written in Chinese characters (''kanji''), the text is according to {{ill|Japanese grammar|en|Japanese grammar|WD=Q1343874|lt=Japanese grammar|1=Japanese grammar}}, and is thus one of the earliest known examples of the adoption of ''kanji'' for use in written Japanese. {{Quotation|{{lang| ja |辛巳歳集月三日記
佐野三家定賜健守命孫黒賣刀自此
新川臣兒斯多々彌足尼孫大兒臣娶生兒
長利僧母爲記定文也　放光寺僧}}}} This stela is listed as being housed in a temple [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Kannon Bosatsu]] by in an inventory compiled by {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu|1=Matsudaira Sadanobu}} in the 1790s. In 1884, the governor of Gunma Prefecture, Katori Motohiko, authorized the prefectural government to purchase a land where the stele is located, put the monument on a new base stone, and had a small structure built to protect it. The current structure dates from 1992, and the stele can be viewed by the public through a window. It is a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Nishi-Yamana Station|en|Nishi-Yamana Station|WD=Q11627819|lt=Nishi-Yamana Station|1=Nishi-Yamana Station}} or {{ill|Yamana Station|en|Yamana Station|WD=Q11467648|lt=Yamana Station|1=Yamana Station}} on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu|Jōshin Dentetsu]] railway. == Family tree == {{familytree/start|style=float:left; font-size:80%; margin:1em;}} {{familytree| 01 | | 02 |01=Tatami-no-sukune
{{lang| ja |多々彌足尼}}|02=Takemori-no-mikoto
{{lang| ja |健守命}}}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 03 | | 04 |03=Niikawa-no-omi
{{lang| ja |新川臣}}|04=unknown}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 05 |y| 06 |05=Ōgo-no-omi
{{lang| ja |大兒臣}} |06=Kurome-no-toji
{{lang| ja |黒賣刀自}}}} {{familytree| | | |!| | | }} {{familytree| | | 07 | | |07=Chōri-no-hōshi
{{lang| ja |長利僧}}}} {{familytree/end}}
==The Yamanoue Kofun== The Yamanoue Kofun is a typical late [[Kofun period]] domed tumulus, located about 15 meters on the east side of the Yamanoue Stele. It has a diameter of 15 meters. Neither ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' nor ''[[fukiishi]]'' have been found at the site. The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele|1=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele|1=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele|1=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele|1=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

[[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[co:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[co:commons:Category:Yamanouehi]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]][[de:Yamanoue no hi]][[en:Yamanoue Stele]][[ja:山ノ上碑]][[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{Infobox ancient site |name = Yamanoue Stele |native_name =山ノ上碑 |alternate_name = |image =Yamanouehi.jpg |alt = |caption = Yamanoue stele | map_type = Japan Gunma Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | map_caption = Yamanoue Stele | map_size = 270px | relief = yes |coordinates = {{coord|36|16|38|N|139|01|40|E|display=inline}} | location = [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki, Gunma]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele|1=Stele}}, ''[[kofun]]'' | part_of = |length = |width = |area = |height = 1.1 meters |builder = |material = {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} |built = 681AD |abandoned = |epochs = {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} |cultures = |dependency_of = |occupants = |event = |excavations = |archaeologists = |condition = | ownership = |management = |public_access = Yes |website = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|Special National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Stele'''|山ノ上碑|Yamanoue hi}} (also written 山上碑) is an {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}} {{ill|Stele|en|Stele|WD=Q178743|lt=Stele|1=Stele}} discovered in the Yamana neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Takasaki, Gunma|Takasaki]], {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma Prefecture|1=Gunma Prefecture}}, in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The stele was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1921, and was raised in status to that of a Special National Historic Site in 1954.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/520 |title= 山上碑及び古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs (Japan) |language=ja |access-date= 11 August 2020}} It is associated with the adjacent {{nihongo|'''Yamanoue Kofun'''|山ノ上古墳||}}, a ''[[kofun]]'' Kofun, which is also covered under the Special Historical Site designation. As one of the "{{ill|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|en|Three Stelae of Kōzuke|WD=Q42426141|lt=Three Stelae of Kōzuke|1=Three Stelae of Kōzuke}}", the Yamanoue Stele was submitted by Japan for inclusion into the {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|WD=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|Memory of the World Programme|en|Memory of the World Programme|WD=Q473858|lt=Memory of the World Programme|1=Memory of the World Programme}} in 2017 {{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/en/communication-and-information/memory-of-the-world/register/full-list-of-registered-heritage/registered-heritage-page-2/three-cherished-stelae-of-ancient-kozuke/ |title= Three Cherished Stelae of Ancient Kozuke|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The inscription on the Yamanoue Stele is the oldest example of writing in Chinese characters according to Japanese grammar. ==The Yamanoue Stele== The practice of erecting stelae was introduced to Japan from the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} and {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang China|1=Tang dynasty}} around the seventh century AD; however, the practice of erecting stelae never became as widespread as in China and Korea, partly due to the fact that in this period of Japanese history, writing was the preserve of the elite. There are only eighteen stelae known to exist from the seventh to eleventh centuries in Japan, of which three are located a small, three-kilometer, area of southwestern Gunma Prefecture. From the fifth century AD, this region welcomed immigrants from the mainland, who brought advanced technologies, such as raising and breeding horses, iron working, stoneware production, and weaving. It is likely that the local inhabitants regarded their region as sophisticated in comparison with surrounding provinces despite its distance from the capital at {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka|1=Asuka, Nara}} or {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}.{{cite web |url= http://www.unesco.org/new/fileadmin/MULTIMEDIA/HQ/CI/CI/pdf/mow/nomination_forms/japan_stelae_eng.pdf|title= Nomination form International Memory of the World Registry|work=Memory of the World |publisher=UNESCO |language=en |access-date= 11 August 2020}} The Yamanoue stele is a natural stone, only slightly worked, reminiscent of similar contemporary stone monuments which have been found in the Korean kingdom of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. The presence of immigrants from Silla in this area and during this time is attested to in the ''[[Shoku Nihongi]]'' official chronicles. The monument is made of {{ill|Andesite|en|Andesite|WD=Q181908|lt=Andesite|1=Andesite}} with a height of 1.11 meters, width of 47 to 50 centimeters and a thickness of approximately. 50 centimeters. The inscription is engraved in {{ill|Clerical script|en|Clerical script|WD=Q1073960|lt=Clerical script|1=Clerical script}} and consists of 53 ''{{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}'' arranged in four vertical rows. It is believed to be an epitaph for the neighboring ''kofun''. From the text it appears that this stele was commissioned by a Buddhist monk named Nagatoshi-no-hoshi from the temple of Hōkō-ji. This monk was a descendant of the local aristocrat who managed the Sano imperial estate, and may be related to the Miyake family mentioned in the nearby {{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele|1=Kanaizawa Stele}}. The monument was erected in honor of his late mother Kurome-tōji. The inscription gives the names of his ancestors on his mother's and father's side, and the date of 681 AD. While the inscription is written in Chinese characters (''kanji''), the text is according to {{ill|Japanese grammar|en|Japanese grammar|WD=Q1343874|lt=Japanese grammar|1=Japanese grammar}}, and is thus one of the earliest known examples of the adoption of ''kanji'' for use in written Japanese. {{Quotation|{{lang| ja |辛巳歳集月三日記
佐野三家定賜健守命孫黒賣刀自此
新川臣兒斯多々彌足尼孫大兒臣娶生兒
長利僧母爲記定文也　放光寺僧}}}} This stela is listed as being housed in a temple [[:en:Avalokitesvara|Kannon Bosatsu]] by in an inventory compiled by {{ill|Matsudaira Sadanobu|en|Matsudaira Sadanobu|WD=Q284038|lt=Matsudaira Sadanobu|1=Matsudaira Sadanobu}} in the 1790s. In 1884, the governor of Gunma Prefecture, Katori Motohiko, authorized the prefectural government to purchase a land where the stele is located, put the monument on a new base stone, and had a small structure built to protect it. The current structure dates from 1992, and the stele can be viewed by the public through a window. It is a 20-minute walk from {{ill|Nishi-Yamana Station|en|Nishi-Yamana Station|WD=Q11627819|lt=Nishi-Yamana Station|1=Nishi-Yamana Station}} or {{ill|Yamana Station|en|Yamana Station|WD=Q11467648|lt=Yamana Station|1=Yamana Station}} on the [[:en:Jōshin Dentetsu|Jōshin Dentetsu]] railway. == Family tree == {{familytree/start|style=float:left; font-size:80%; margin:1em;}} {{familytree| 01 | | 02 |01=Tatami-no-sukune
{{lang| ja |多々彌足尼}}|02=Takemori-no-mikoto
{{lang| ja |健守命}}}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 03 | | 04 |03=Niikawa-no-omi
{{lang| ja |新川臣}}|04=unknown}} {{familytree| |!| | | |!| }} {{familytree| 05 |y| 06 |05=Ōgo-no-omi
{{lang| ja |大兒臣}} |06=Kurome-no-toji
{{lang| ja |黒賣刀自}}}} {{familytree| | | |!| | | }} {{familytree| | | 07 | | |07=Chōri-no-hōshi
{{lang| ja |長利僧}}}} {{familytree/end}}
==The Yamanoue Kofun== The Yamanoue Kofun is a typical late [[Kofun period]] domed tumulus, located about 15 meters on the east side of the Yamanoue Stele. It has a diameter of 15 meters. Neither ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'' nor ''[[fukiishi]]'' have been found at the site. The tomb has an opening to the south, with a total length of 7.4 meters, with a [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] measuring 2.68 by 1.75 meters with a 1.66 meter height. The tomb has been open since antiquity and no {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} have been found. It is possibly from the same date, or slightly later than the stele, and from the inscription on the Yamanoue Stele, it is assumed that it was the tomb of Kurome-tōji. In addition, there is another ''kofun'' located 200 meters to the west of this tumulus, of the same scale and approximate age. Called the Yamanokami Nishi Kofun, this ''kofun'' has collapsed and is mostly buried, and is not part of the historic site designation. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|WD=Q16248234|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Gunma)}} *{{ill|Kanaizawa Stele|en|Kanaizawa Stele|WD=Q11646374|lt=Kanaizawa Stele|1=Kanaizawa Stele}} *{{ill|Tago Stele|en|Tago Stele|WD=Q11431029|lt=Tago Stele|1=Tago Stele}} ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category:kk]] }} [[file:山ノ上古墳.JPG|thumb|right|250px|Yamanoue Kofun]] [[en:Yamanoue Stele]] [[az:Yamanoe stelası]] {{Commons category|Yamanouehi}} [[de:Yamanoue no hi]] [[ja:山ノ上碑]] [[th:ศิลาจารึกยามาโนอูเอะ]] {{wikidata link|Q11465812}} [[Category:681 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century inscriptions]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:History of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:Memory of the World Register]] [[Category:Special Historic Sites]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Steles in Japan]] [[Category:Takasaki, Gunma]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
